coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive and stable sensor for the detection of capsaicin using electrocatalytic carbon dots grafted onto indium tin oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Thongsai, Nichaphat
Wei, Wei
Gopalan, Padma
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a highly sensitive and stable capsaicin sensor was developed from carbon dots synthesized from iota-carrageenan. The carbon dots were grafted onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate via a novel one-step thermal annealing process. This was used as an electrode for the electrochemical detection of capsaicin. The carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode was sensitive across a wide linear range from 0.05 to 500 μM, with a remarkably low detection limit of 5.4 nM. The electrode showed excellent recyclability over 450 cycles, suggesting that it was electrochemically stable and can be reused for several hundred times. It demonstrated good selectivity towards capsaicin in the presence of multiple interferences: d-(+)-glucose, citric acid, l-ascorbic acid, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Cl−. A recovery test was performed using Cayenne pepper, Tabasco sauce, and Thai chili, yielding the recovery in the range 92–108 %. In addition to high sensitivity, stability, and selectivity, the sensor could produce the detection results within 5 min. The novel capsaicin sensor developed here has potential uses for quality control in the agricultural, food, and pharmaceutical sectors.

(a) TEM image of carbon dots; inset figure shows magnified view of carbon dot and lattice spacing and (b) histogram of size distribution.

(a) UV–vis absorbance spectrum and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots.

Survey XPS spectra of bare ITO (red line, bottom) and carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate (black line, top). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of carbon dot-grafted ITO and bare ITO in 0.1 M Na2SO4 in the presence of 50 μM capsaicin and cyclic voltammogram of the carbon dot-grafted ITO in 0.1 M Na2SO4 in the absence of capsaicin at a scan rate of 50 mV∙s−1 (first scan = dash line and second scan = solid line). (b) Schematic representation of electrochemical oxidation/reduction of capsaicin.

Inset shows a plot of peak potential and pH.

(a) Plot of peak I current and accumulation time of carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate. (b) Cyclic voltammograms of carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate in 0.1 M Na2SO4 (pH = 2) in the presence of 50 μM capsaicin at a scan rate of 100 mV∙s−1. Inset shows a plot of peak current retention against number of cycles. (c) Cyclic voltammograms of carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate in freshly made capsaicin-containing electrolyte. (d) Plot of peak current retention against number of cycles.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of carbon dot-grafted ITO in 0.1 M Na2SO4 in the presence of 30 μM capsaicin and interferences at a scan rate of 50 mV∙s−1. (b) Relative current (%) versus interference compounds. (c) HPLC chromatograms of standard capsaicin and extracts from real samples. Inset shows the calibration curve of peak areas against concentrations of capsaicinoids. (d) Cyclic voltammograms of carbon dot-grafted ITO in the presence of extracts from real samples.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive and stable sensor for the detection of capsaicin using electrocatalytic carbon dots grafted onto indium tin oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a highly sensitive and stable capsaicin sensor was developed from carbon dots synthesized from iota-carrageenan. The carbon dots were grafted onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate via a novel one-step thermal annealing process. This was used as an electrode for the electrochemical detection of capsaicin. The carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode was sensitive across a wide linear range from 0.05 to 500 μM, with a remarkably low detection limit of 5.4 nM. The electrode showed excellent recyclability over 450 cycles, suggesting that it was electrochemically stable and can be reused for several hundred times. It demonstrated good selectivity towards capsaicin in the presence of multiple interferences: d-(+)-glucose, citric acid, l-ascorbic acid, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Cl−. A recovery test was performed using Cayenne pepper, Tabasco sauce, and Thai chili, yielding the recovery in the range 92–108 %. In addition to high sensitivity, stability, and selectivity, the sensor could produce the detection results within 5 min. The novel capsaicin sensor developed here has potential uses for quality control in the agricultural, food, and pharmaceutical sectors.

ce:section.ce:para: Spicy foods are widely consumed around the world. Their popularity is mainly due to the presence of capsaicinoids [1]. Capsaicinoids are a class of organic compound, capable of creating heat, pungent flavor, and an endorphin rush that many people find pleasing [2]. Capsaicin (8-methyl-N-vanillyl-6-nonenamide), a derivative of benzylamine, is the major compound in the capsaicinoid family, accounting for nearly 70 % of total capsaicinoids in spices [3]. The amount of capsaicin is an important quality indicator for commercial chili and other peppers. Many people consider capsaicin to be the king of medicinal herbs because they believe it brings many health benefits. Capsaicin has been reported to enhance metabolism and digestive system health, and reduce cholesterol and the risk of stomach ulcers [4,5]. It is also a potent antioxidant, anti-bacterial, and anti-carcinogen [6,7]. In addition, capsaicin is useful in controlling chemotherapy- and radiotherapy-induced mucositis [8]. However, many people are allergic to capsaicin, suffering irritation, lip swelling, nasal congestion, bloating, nausea, diarrhea, etc [9]. The development of detection methods for capsaicin using simple, low-cost, and accurate approaches is scientifically and technologically important for the food, herbal, health, and pharmaceutical industries.

The analysis of capsaicin can be performed using various methods. The Scoville organoleptic test is commonly used as an industrial standard for measuring the degree of spiciness. However, it is subjective because it relies on volunteer tasters. There are also advanced techniques for quantitatively determining the capsaicin level including gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, colorimetry, capillary electrophoresis, and mass spectroscopy [2,10–13]. However, these techniques require either costly instruments, analytical resources, time-consuming measurement, multi-step sample preparation, or specially-trained technicians. Electrochemical techniques are attractive alternatives having the advantage of relatively cheap instruments, and being usable for on-site testing [14]. However, they are rarely used for the detection of capsaicin. The detection of capsaicin via electrochemical techniques relies upon redox reactions between capsaicin and specially designed electrodes. For example, Ya et al. used mesoporous silica and amino-functionalized mesoporous silica as composite electrodes to detect capsaicin with a detection limit of 0.02 μM and 93.1–100.7 % spiked recovery [15]. Similarly, Randviir et al. fabricated screen-printed carbon nanotube electrodes and used them to detect capsaicin via both cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, yielding a detection limit of 0.31 μM [16]. Kim et al. reported the use of a glassy carbon modified with graphene-doped sol-gel titania-Nafion composite film as the working electrode, providing an outstandingly low detection limit of 8.6 nM [17]. These electrochemical methods were demonstrated to be highly sensitive towards capsaicin. However, the electrode materials are expensive and fabrication methods are rather complex. The search for development of cheaper or simpler materials is important.

Carbon dots are optically and electronically unique nanomaterials with uses as sensing probes for the detection of various ions and chemicals. They possess carbon cores with surface functional groups, endowing them with unique optical, electronic, and sensing properties [18]. The interactions between these surface functional groups and chemicals or ions alter the electronic properties, and these are readily detected using electrochemical instruments. The use of carbon dots as electrochemical probes for the detection of analytes has been reported, including catechol [19], adrenaline [20], dopamine [21], amoxicillin [22], mercury [23], paracetamol [24], nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide [25], bisphenol S [26], and hydrogen peroxide [27]. Carbon dots are attractive sensing probes because of their unique interactions and excellent selectivity towards trace amounts of analytes. However, to the best of our knowledge, the use of carbon dots as electrochemical probes for capsaicin sensing has not been reported.

In this work, novel electrochemical probes prepared from carbon dots for the detection of capsaicin were developed (Fig. 1 ). The carbon dots were synthesized from iota-carrageenan using a one-step hydrothermal method. Carrageenan is an abundant food ingredient obtained from seaweed [28]. It is composed of repeating galactose residues. There are three primary classes of carrageenan depending on the degree of sulfonation: one, two, and three sulfate groups per disaccharide for kappa-, iota-, and lambda-carrageenan, respectively [29]. Iota-carrageenan was chosen because it is the cheapest. For the fabrication of electrodes, the carbon dots were then grafted onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate. Suzuki et al. described the grafting of carbon dots using a two-step silane strategy [30]. It is well known that silane linkers are prone to multilayer formation and hydrolysis, leading to the delamination of the grafted layer [31]. Herein, a one-step thermal annealing method was developed for the first time for grafting the carbon dots onto the ITO substrate. The carbon dots contained hydroxyl groups that thermally condensed with the hydroxyl groups of the treated ITO surface. The formation of ether linkage by thermal annealing has been reported to produce a stable grafted layer of bipyridine molecules on oxide surfaces [32]. The electrochemical properties of the carbon dot-grafted ITO were measured using cyclic voltammetry (CV). The effects of capsaicin concentration, scan rate, pH, and accumulation time were investigated. The recyclability of electrode and detection of capsaicin in Cayenne pepper, Tabasco sauce, and chili were also studied. In summary, the novelty of this work lies in the synthesis of carbon dots from iota-carrageenan, the grafting of carbon dots by thermal annealing on ITO, and the use of carbon dots as highly sensitive, selective, and stable electrochemical sensors for fast capsaicin detection. This paper presents new facets of carbonaceous nanomaterials and their uses in sensing applications. In addition to sensing application reported in this paper, the versatile strategy can be also applied to grafting of carbon dots onto other oxide substrates, for a wider range of applications.

Capsaicin (analytical standard and natural grade), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), and methanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Acetonitrile was of HPLC grade obtained from Carlo Erba. Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) and ethanol were purchased from Fisher Scientific. Cayenne pepper, Tabasco sauce, and chili were purchased from a local supermarket. A dialysis membrane, with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da, was bought from Membrane Filtration Products, Inc. Unpolished float glass SiO2 passivated with indium tin oxide (Rs = 70–100 Ω) was purchased from Delta Technologies Limited. The capsaicin stock solution was prepared in ethanol, the others in deionized (DI) water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from iota-carrageenan
ce:section.ce:para: Iota-carrageenan (2 g), hydrogen peroxide solution (3% v/v, 20 mL), and DI water (80 mL) were placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The solution was filtered, neutralized with 10 M NaOH solution, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The carbon dot solution was further purified using the dialysis membrane for 72 h and freeze dried to yield the brown solid product of carbon dots (0.0885 g, 4.43 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grafting of carbon dots onto ITO substrate
ce:section.ce:para: ITO substrates were cleaned by immersing in H2O2: NH4OH: H2O solution (1:1:5 by volume) and heated at 80 °C for 30 min. The substrates were then rinsed with DI water and methanol and dried in a vacuum oven. The carbon dot aqueous solution (100 μL, 10 g L−1) was drop-casted onto the cleaned ITO substrates (1 × 2 cm2) and allowed to dry in a vacuum oven for 1 h. The substrates were then thermally annealed at 160 °C for 12 h, 18 h, and 24 h in a vacuum chamber. After the annealing, the substrates were rinsed several times with DI water, then ultrasonicated in DI water (3 × 5 min) to remove unreacted carbon dots. The carbon dot-grafted substrates were dried under N2 and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

The carbon dots were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Spectrum 2000, Perkin Elmer). The elemental composition was investigated using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical). The size and morphology of carbon dots were obtained using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai™ G2 F20, FEI Corporation). The zeta potential was measured using a nanoparticle analyzer (Nano Partica SZ-100, Horiba). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1700 PharmaSpec, Shimadzu) and a fluorescence spectrometer (FP-6200, Jasco), respectively. The surface morphology and topography of the carbon dots grafted on ITO substrate were measured using an atomic force microscope (Bruker Multimode 8) in tapping mode.

The relative quantum yields of the carbon dots were measured based on our previous studies [33,34]. Quinine sulfate was used as a reference. The optical absorbance and emission of carbon dots and quinine sulfate were measured, and the quantum yield was calculated using the slope method.

An electrochemical cell was assembled in a three-electrode system: the carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate as working electrode, Ag/AgCl (3 M NaCl) as reference electrode, and platinum (Pt) as counter electrode. A stock solution of capsaicin (200 μM) was prepared in ethanol. The electrolyte was prepared by mixing the capsaicin and 0.1 M Na2SO4 solutions and the pH adjusted using HCl or NaOH. The carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate was then immersed in the electrolyte. The electrochemical response was measured using a potentiostat (SP-200, BioLogic Science Instruments) in a potential range between -0.1 and 1.3 V.

Cayenne pepper (red pepper powder), Tabasco sauce, and Thai chili were chosen as real samples to test the practicality and accuracy of the sensor. They were dissolved in ethanol (25 mL) and the mixtures were ultrasonicated for 1 h and then stirred for a further 2 h. They were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min to remove solid residues. The supernatants (20 mL) were collected and diluted with ethanol (140 mL). The extracts were also spiked with three concentrations of standard capsaicin (natural grade). Two standard methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and UV–vis spectroscopy, were used for the validation of our sensor. For the HPLC measurement (Shimadzu, LC-20AT series), a UV detector (222 nm) and C18 column (4.6 mm i.d. × 150 mm, 5 μm particle diameter) joined with a guard column (4.6 mm i.d. × 12.5 mm, 5 μm particle diameter) were used with acetonitrile/water mixture (50:50) as mobile phase. The mobile phase and the samples were filtered through 0.45- and 0.22-μm nylon membranes, respectively. The samples were then injected into a 10-μL loop feeding of the machine at ambient temperature with a flow rate 1.0 ml min−1 and a 10-minute analysis time. A calibration curve was constructed based on standard capsaicin solutions in ethanol (5, 10, 15, 20, 50, 75, 100 mg L-1). The amounts of capsaicin in the Cayenne pepper, Tabasco sauce, and chili were then determined and the recovery in percentage was calculated using Eq. 1.

An interference test was carried out using d-(+)-glucose, citric acid, l-ascorbic acid, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Cl− as interferences. Capsaicin (30 μM) was mixed with each of the interferences (30 μM) in Na2SO4 (0.1 M, 60 mL, pH = 2), and this was used as the electrolyte for CV measurement. A control sample was prepared without the addition of interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots from iota-carrageenan
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were synthesized using iota-carrageenan as the carbon source. Iota-carrageenan is a linear polysaccharide with two sulfate groups per disaccharide. Due to its high polarity, it is highly solvated with water and therefore suitable for synthesis of carbon dots under aqueous conditions. In this work, carbon dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal process. This is a simple, convenient, and green method [35] and can also produce a high yield. Hydrogen peroxide was used as a catalyst based on the synthesis of carbon dots from sodium alginate [36]. Hydrogen peroxide, the simplest peroxide, can break down into reactive hydroxyl radicals that can readily react with the precursor and create hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of resulting carbon dots [37]. Iota-carrageenan was subjected to a hydrothermal process at 220 °C for 12 h. After centrifugation, dialysis, and freeze drying, carbon dots were obtained.

The morphology and size of carbon dots were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 2 a). The TEM image exhibited scattered quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm and a size distribution from 2 to 6 nm with an average of 3.6 nm (Fig. 2b). The zeta potential was measured to be -45.5 mV. The highly negative zeta potential is attributed to the presence of oxygen atoms and sulfate anions. The highly negative zeta potential agreed with the carbon dots to have a good dispersion of nanoparticles, consistent with the TEM image.

The surface functional groups were examined using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, a broad vibration band was observed at 3413 cm−1, representing OH stretching vibration. The vibration bands at 2929, 1625, and 1116 cm−1 were attributed to CH, CO, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [38–40]. The signal at 1382 cm−1 corresponded to CH3 bending vibration [41]. These results suggested that carbon dots contained hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups, and possibly carboxyl groups, enhancing the solubility of the carbon dots in aqueous solution. The chemical composition of the carbon dots was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. 3b). The XPS survey spectrum demonstrated intense signals from C1s and O1s at 285.9 eV and 531.9 eV with atomic concentrations of 65.39 % and 25.04 %, respectively [42,43]. The S2p signal was found at 168.1 eV [44]. The high resolution of C1s exhibited signals at 284.9, 286.3, 287.8, and 289 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, COC, CO, and OC O bonds, respectively (Fig. 3c) [43,45,46]. High-resolution peaks of O1s located at 531, 532.1, 533.1, 534.4, and 536.2 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, COH, O CO, and COC functionalities, respectively (Fig. 3d) [43,47]. These characteristics were in good agreement with the FT-IR and XPS results, confirming the presence of carbon- and oxygen-containing functional groups and sp2 carbon.

The carbon dots were characterized to determine their optical absorption and emission using UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum exhibited strong absorption in the UV region (200−300 nm) with a small shoulder at 270 nm, is corresponding to the π-π* transition from the aromatic sp2 carbons in the carbon dots. The optical absorption tailed off into the visible region up to 600 nm (Fig. 4 a). The fluorescence emission spectra were determined as a function of excitation wavelength and displayed excitation-dependent emission. The fluorescence emission spectra shifted slightly when the excitation wavelength was increased from 340 to 500 nm. Under irradiation by 360 nm UV light, the carbon dot solution provided maximum emission at 472 nm (Fig. 4b). The relative quantum efficiency was determined to be 2 % using quinine sulfate as reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the carbon dot-grafted ITO
ce:section.ce:para: Indium tin oxide (ITO) is commonly used as a transparent electrode in optoelectronics applications because of its high electrical conductivity and wide electrochemical working window [48]. In this study, we used carbon dot-grafted ITO as an electrode for the electrochemical detection of capsaicin. A novel one-step thermal annealing method was developed for the preparation of the carbon dot-grafted ITO. Since our carbon dots contain hydroxyl group, as shown by the FT-IR and XPS analysis, they could be grafted onto the ITO substrate. Upon thermal annealing at 160 °C, the condensation between the hydroxyl groups of the molecules and the surface oxides formed ether linkages (Fig. 5 a) [32,49,50]. After thermal annealing, the samples were cleaned thoroughly to remove any unbound carbon dots.

The surface topography of the carbon dot-grafted ITO was investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 5 shows the AFM images of the bare ITO substrate and the carbon dot-grafted ITO substrates from thermal annealing times of 12, 18, and 24 h. The AFM image of bare ITO showed no particles on the substrate and a relatively smooth surface with an RMS roughness of 0.74 nm (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c–e, large spherical particles were observed after grafting. This indicated the successful grafting of the carbon dots onto the ITO substrate. The cross-sectional images showed particle heights of approximately 2−5 nm. The 12-h annealing sample exhibited larger particles and a higher RMS roughness whereas the 18-h and 24-h annealing samples showed smaller particles and lower RMS roughness. This suggested that 18 h annealing produced a complete grafting of the carbon dots onto the ITO surface. The large particles on the 12-h sample suggested that grafting had not completed. These results suggested that the thermal annealing time governed the grafting of carbon dots, and that 18 h was the optimal annealing time.

The chemical compositions of the bare and carbon dot-grafted ITO samples were analyzed using XPS (Fig. 6 ). The survey XPS spectrum of the bare ITO showed four major peaks of O1s, C1s, In3d, and In4d at 532.08, 284.08, 445.08 and 19.08 eV [51]. The survey XPS spectrum of the 18 h carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate showed attenuation of the indium peaks and a more prominent C1s signal than the bare ITO. The AFM and XPS results confirmed that carbon dots were successfully grafted onto the ITO substrate.

It is well known that capsaicin can undergo redox reactions and therefore can be detected using electrochemical techniques [15,52]. The electrochemical properties of the carbon dot-grafted and bare ITO samples in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte were studied using cyclic voltammetry in the absence and presence of capsaicin (50 μM). Na2SO4 electrolyte was chosen because it is mild, inert, and widely used in electrochemical systems such as sensors, batteries, and supercapacitors [53]. To investigate the electrochemical reactions of capsaicin using the carbon dot-grafted ITO, the two successive CV scans were performed at a scan rate of 50 mV∙s−1 and a voltage between -0.1 and 1.3 V. The cyclic voltammogram of bare ITO as the control sample in the capsaicin-containing electrolyte showed very small peaks at 0.23 and 1.17 V. The carbon dot-grafted ITO sample in the absence of capsaicin also exhibited no peaks. In contrast, the carbon dot-grafted ITO sample in the capsaicin-containing electrolyte showed two pronounced peaks at 0.30 and 1.09 V in the first scan and three peaks at 0.26, 0.83, and 1.12 V in the second scan (Fig. 7 a). The enhanced peak currents and slight peak shifts compared with the control samples indicated that the carbon dots facilitated the redox reactions of the capsaicin.

The cyclic voltammogram of the carbon dot-grafted ITO in the presence of capsaicin can be explained as follows. As shown in Fig. 7, peak I at 1.12 V corresponded to the two-electron irreversible oxidation of capsaicin and the hydrolysis of the 2-methoxy group to form o-benzoquinone. Peak II at 0.26 V corresponded to the two-electron reduction of the o-benzoquinone to catechol, whereas peak III at 0.83 V corresponded to the reverse process, the two-electron oxidation reaction of catechol to o-benzoquinone. Peak III was not observed in the first scan due to the absence of catechol, which would be formed after reduction of o-benzoquinone (peak II). This means that two successive scans must be performed to detect all three peaks. The signal of peak I in the second scan was lower than that in the first scan due to the depletion of capsaicin in the solution and the inhibition of electroactive surface by the products of capsaicin oxidation. These three peaks belong to the characteristic redox reactions of capsaicin [17,52,54]. In the absence of carbon dots, there were trace redox peaks, suggesting that the carbon dots electrocatalyzed the redox reactions of capsaicin by facilitating electron transfer.

We next investigated the effect of the pH of the electrolyte on the sensing of capsaicin by the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode. Three carbon dot-grafted ITO samples were prepared by thermal annealing for 12 h, 18 h, and 24 h. Fig. 8 a shows the relationship between peak I current and pH. The 18-h sample gave the highest currents at all pH values and was therefore used in all further experiments. This was attributed to the fact that the 18-h sample had the most uniform and smooth surface, encouraging interaction and electron transfer between the electrode and the capsaicin. Acidic conditions yielded higher peak currents than basic conditions because in the latter the o-methoxyphenol moiety in the capsaicin can be deprotonated to form o-methoxy phenoxide. Upon electro-oxidation, the phenoxide can turn into phenoxy radical, which is prone to dimerization. The deprotonation and dimerization are side reactions that lower the concentration of capsaicin, consequently lowering the current signals at high pH [15,52]. As shown in Fig. 8b, the oxidation peak potential also shifted to lower potential values as the pH value increased. The peak shift with respect to pH can be used to investigate electron transfer using Nernst equation (Eq. 3):

The linear relationship between the peak I potential (Epa1 ) and pH values (pH 1–5) yielded the regression equation: Epa1(V) = 1.2885−0.0587pH (R2 = 0.9799), as shown in the inset of Fig. 8b. The slope of -0.0587 is close to the ideal value of -0.0592 [55,56]. These results suggested that equal numbers of electrons and protons were transferred in the redox reaction of capsaicin, consistent with the mechanism described above.

The effects of scan rate were investigated using the carbon dot-grafted ITO in 0.1 M Na2SO4 (pH = 2). The peak currents of all three peaks increased as in the scan rate (20–300 mV∙s−1) increased (Fig. 9 a). This increased the charging current applied to the system, leading to a reduction in the diffusion layer, so that higher peak currents were observed [57]. To determine the number of electrons involved in the process, electron transfer was analyzed using Laviron’s equation (Eq. 4) [17,54]:

where Epa is the anodic peak potential, E° is the formal redox potential, α is the electron transfer coefficient, which is assumed to be 0.5 in irreversible electrode process, n is the number of electrons transferred, ks is the standard electron transfer rate constant of the reaction, R is the universal gas constant (R = 8.314 J∙K−1 mol−1), T is the temperature (298 K), F is Faraday’s constant (96,485 C∙mol−1), and V is the scan rate (mV∙s−1). The peak I potentials (Epa1 ) were used to fit Laviron’s equation as shown in Fig. 9b. The regression equation obtained was Epa1 = 0.0277ln V + 1.1045 (R 2 = 0.99). The slope (
R T

) was determined to be 0.0277, and therefore the n value obtained was 1.85 or close to 2. These results were good agreement with previous reports that assumed the oxidation reaction of capsaicin to be a two-electron transfer process [15,17,54]. This indicated that the sensing mechanism of the carbon dot-grafted ITO occurred via the redox reactions of capsaicin and the carbon dots, which facilitated two-electron transfer.

A linear relationship between the scan rate and peak current also suggests either quasi-reversible or irreversible process (Fig. S1) [58]. To determine whether the electron transfer is a quasi-reversible or irreversible process, the relationship between the standard electron-transfer rate constant (k o), mass transport (mtran
s), and scan rate (V) was examined. First, the diffusion coefficient (Do ) value was calculated to be 1.56 × 10−5 cm2·s-1 using Wike-Chang equation (Eq. 5) [59]:

The determination of ko and mtrans were then carried out using Gileadi’s equation (Eq. 6) and Eq.7, respectively [58,61]:

The ko and mtrans values were calculated to be 6.65 × 10−3 cm·s-1 and 1.33 × 10-2 cm·s (at a scan rate of 0.05 V·s-1). Based on the Nicholsan and Shain theory, ko in the range between 0.35·V 1/2 and 0.35 × 10-4·V 1/2 indicates the quasi-reversible process [58]. Furthermore, ko and mtrans are in the similar order of magnitude, which also confirms that the electron transfer is the quasi-reversible process.

The effects of scan rate on peak current were further investigated to determine whether capsaicin sensing occurred via diffusion or adsorption. Based on the previous work by Naghian et al., the logarithm plot of peak current and scan rate was constructed [62]. All three peak currents were found to be linearly proportional with the scan rate (Fig. 9c). This confirms that both adsorption and diffusion contributed to the electron transfer processes between capsaicin and the carbon dot-grafted ITO substrate. To determine which mechanism is more dominant in controlling the sensing of capsaicin of the electrode, the peak currents (ip ) and the square root of scan rates (V 1/2) were plotted based on the quasi-reversible Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. 8) to determine whether the analyte was freely diffusing in solution (Fig. 9d) [57]:

For an electrode-adsorbed species, the peak currents and scan rates shown in Fig. S1 were plotted based on Eq. 9 [57]:

As the coefficients of determination (R2) in Fig. 9d were almost similar to those in Fig. S1, we concluded that both diffusion and adsorption controlled the sensing of capsaicin by the carbon dot-grafted electrode. The electroactive area (Areal ) of the carbon dot-grafted electrode was next determined using Eq. 8. The ratio between Areal and Ageo (geometrical area of the electrode, 2 cm2) was calculated using Eq. 10 and given as %Real [63]:
(10)

) × 100
Areal and %Real were found to be 0.47 cm2 and 23.5 %, respectively. This means about a quarter of the electrode surface participated in the electron transfer reaction.

From the above results, the carbon dots increased the current response by facilitating electron transfer in the electrochemical reactions of capsaicin. Based on previous reports, we attributed the electrocatalytic properties of the carbon dots to the highest-occupied and lowest- unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of the carbon dots (-7.13 eV and -3.52 eV) [64], capsaicin (-5.93 eV and -0.49 eV) [65], p-benzoquinone (-7.35 eV and -3.48 eV) [66], and catechol derivative (-5.68 eV and -0.01 eV) [67] (Fig. 10 ). When a positive voltage was applied the potential energy of the electrode dropped, favoring electron transfer from the capsaicin (peak I) and catechol (peak III) to the electrode. Conversely, when a negative voltage was applied, the potential energy of the electrode increased, becoming energetically favorable for the electrons to transfer from the electrode to the benzoquinone (peak II). A layer of grafted carbon dots with favorable energy levels therefore may have facilitated electron transfer from capsaicin and catechol to electrode and from electrode to benzoquinone, leading to the enhanced peak currents. All these chemical species must also be in proximity to the electrode for electron transfer. Favorable interactions may take place between the carbon dots and the diffusing chemical species. It has been reported that carbon-based nanomaterials enhance the adsorption/diffusion of capsaicin on electrodes [17,52]. These synergistic effects therefore explain the enhanced electron transfer and current response, and sensitivity to capsaicin of the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode.

To further examine detection by the carbon dot-grafted ITO, a quantitative analysis was performed using thirteen concentrations of capsaicin (0.05–500 μM) in 0.1 M Na2SO4 (pH = 2). The cyclic voltammograms obtained showed larger signals as the capsaicin concentration increased (Fig. 11 a). When plotted against the capsaicin concentration, the peak I, II, and III currents showed a linear relationship. However, the peak I values showed the widest linear range from 0.05 to 500 μM, and were therefore, used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD) (Fig. 11b). The limit of detection (3 σ / s l o p e , where σ is the standard deviation of the peak current) was calculated to be 5.4 nM, which is equivalent to 0.0016 ppm. Compared with previous reports (Table 1 ), our linear range and detection limit were among the best. A pencil-graphite electrode [68] and AuNPs-MWCNT-GCE [69] showed slightly lower detection limits, but yielded a much narrower linear range, which may limit their practical applications. Lau et al. reported a wide linear range of 100−10,000 μM, but their sensor was only suitable for high capsaicin concentrations (millimolar range) and the detection limit was not reported [70]. Our sensor, however, produced a wide linear range and at the same time gave a low detection limit, suitable for the detection of a wide range of capsaicin concentrations. The inset of Fig. 11b shows a linear relationship between the peak I current and Scoville units (1 ppm is approximately equal to 15 Scoville units) [16]. A linear equation was obtained with a regression of y = 0.00008×Scoville units + 0.00167 (R2 = 0.9933). We thus demonstrated that the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode can also be used to quantify the degree of spiciness in Scoville units.

The effect of accumulation time on capsaicin sensing of carbon dot-grafted ITO was next investigated. The carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode was immersed in an electrolyte solution for a predetermined time prior to cyclic voltammetry measurement. The values of peak I current rapidly increased as the accumulation time was increased from 0 to 4 min. The peak current then plateaued (Fig. 12 a). A cycle of CV measurement took about 1 min, meaning that our sensor could produce the detection results in less than 5 min. This is much shorter than other previously reported methods, such as gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, mass spectrometry, and colorimetry, which usually require 10−40 min of measurement time [2,10,11,13]. The use of a carbon dot-grafted electrode was thus demonstrated to be a fast, low-cost detection method that can give results within a few minutes. The accumulation time dependence also indicated that the sensing was due to the diffusion and surface adsorption of capsaicin, as the capsaicin must migrate to the carbon dot-grafted electrode and adsorb on the surface prior to the redox reactions.

The recyclability of the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode in capsaicin-containing electrolyte was next examined (Fig. 12b). We found that the currents of peak I, II, and III remained above 80 % after 200 cycles and above 60 % after 350 cycles. The decrease in current response was mainly due to the depletion of capsaicin in the electrolyte. To verify that the decrease in current response was indeed due to the capsaicin depletion, we then used fresh capsaicin-containing electrolyte for every cycle. It was found that the recyclability of the CV signals was much improved (Fig. 12c). The peak currents remained above 80 % after 450 cycles and could still be clearly observed even after 600 cycles (Fig. 12d). These results indicated that our carbon dot-grafted ITO sample was electrochemically stable and our sensor can be reused for at least 450 cycles. To confirm the reusability, the peak currents were measured in different concentrations of capsaicin using the used electrode that had been tested for 450 cycles. The linear dependence between capsaicin concentration and peak current was still obtained, yielding a detection limit of 30.3 nM (Fig. S2). This indicates that the carbon dot-grafted electrode has excellent stability and reusability. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the best recyclabilities of electrochemical sensors reported to date. This was attributed to the stability of the carbon dots and grafted layer, which is one of the advantages of surface grafting.

An interference study was performed to test the selectivity of the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode. Ions and chemicals used as interference compounds were Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cl−, d-(+)-glucose, citric acid, and l-ascorbic acid because they are ubiquitous. They were added to the electrolyte along with the capsaicin at the same concentration. As can be seen from Fig. 13 a, the cyclic voltammograms still showed three peaks of redox reactions of capsaicin. These inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, and Cl−), and the d-(+)-glucose and citric acid showed negligible interfering effects in the cyclic voltammograms. Compared with the control, the relative peak currents in the presence of interferences were in the range 90–109 % (Fig. 13b). The relative oxidation currents of peak I and III of l-ascorbic acid were approximately 140 % of the control. This suggested that the l-ascorbic acid underwent electrochemical reactions under the applied voltages, contributing to the higher current response. l-ascorbic acid can be oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid [71], which was confirmed by the fact that only oxidation peak I and III increased, but the reduction peak II was not altered. We therefore suggest the use of peak II or selection of suitable extracting solvents for capsaicin analysis when l-ascorbic acid may be present to minimize the interfering effects. Overall, the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode was highly sensitive and selective to capsaicin, a making it suitable for practical applications.

To investigate the accuracy and reliability of our electrochemical method, the carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode was used for capsaicin sensing in real samples: Cayenne pepper, Tabasco sauce, and chili and tested against the HPLC and UV–vis techniques. Since these real samples contain capsaicin and other capsaicinoid compounds and no further purification was carried out, the total amount of capsaisinoids will be reported in this experiment. First, three real samples were validated using HPLC techniques. Using acetonitrile/water mixture as eluent, the extracts showed signals at 3.08 and 3.60 min, corresponding to capsaicin and dihydrocapsaicin, respectively (Fig. 13c) [72]. From the voltammograms, the CD-grafted ITO electrode exhibited all three peaks (I, II, and III), also confirming that the extracts contained the capsaicinoids (Fig. 13d). As shown in Table 2 , the recovery and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were found to be 92–106 % and less than 3.8 %, respectively, indicating that our electrochemical method for the determination of capsaicinoids in real samples had good accuracy and reliability. We also used the UV–vis technique as another validation method. The real samples also exhibited absorption peaks at 281 nm, confirming the presence of capsaicinoids (Fig. S3). The recovery and RSDs were 96–108 % and below 4.5 %, respectively, thereby confirming the accuracy and reliability. The ascorbic acid that is usually present in chili did not significantly affect the detection results because ascorbic acid possesses very low solubility in ethanol, the extracting solvent used. As validated by both HPLC and UV–vis techniques, this demonstrated that our electrochemical method is sufficiently accurate and reliable for detection of capsaicinoids in real samples. This also implies that the detection of capsaicin would also be accurate and reliable.

In this work, a highly sensitive, selective, and stable sensor for the fast detection of capsaicin using the carbon dot-grafted ITO was successfully demonstrated. The carbon dots were synthesized from iota-carrageenan and then grafted onto ITO by a novel thermal annealing method. The carbon dot-grafted electrode was sensitive to capsaicin, yielding one of the widest linear ranges of 0.05–500 μM, one of the lowest detection limits of 5.4 nM or 0.0016 ppm, and high selectivity to capsaicin in the presence of interferences. The peak currents remained above 80 % after 450 cycles, indicating excellent recyclability and multiple reuses. The concentrations of capsaicin in real samples were accurately quantified. Electrochemical characterization and data fitting elucidated the sensing mechanisms that the detection of capsaicin took place via two-electron transfer. The carbon dots facilitated electron transfer between capsaicin and electrode due to synergistically favorable energy levels and enhanced interactions. This work is the first to report the use of electrocatalytic carbon dots as highly sensitive and selective capsaicin sensors. The novel capabilities of these carbon nanomaterials may have a wide range of practical applications.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Preeyanuch Supchocksoonthorn is currently a Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University since 2017. Her current interest in research focuses on carbon nanomaterials and electrochemical sensor.

Nichaphat Thongsai is a Ph.D. graduate from the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University. She is now working as a postdoctoral researcher at the National Metal and Materials Technology Center, Thailand.

Wei Wei is currently as an engineer at Intel Corporation. He received his Ph.D. from the Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison, in 2019. His research interests focus on surface modification using polymer brushes.

Padma Gopalan is a professor at the Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison. She obtained her Ph.D. at Cornell University, in 2001. During 2001–2003, she worked as a postdoctoral fellow at Bell Laboratories, Lucent Technologies. Her research interests include polymer synthesis and characterization, electro-optic and photonic materials, self-assembly of block copolymers, photonic devices, and liquid crystalline polymers.

Peerasak Paoprasert is currently an associate professor at Thammasat University. He received his B.A. from Northwestern University in 2005 and Ph.D. from University of Wisconsin-Madison in 2010. He joined Thammasat University in 2011 and has been working on the design and synthesis of functional carbon materials.
